Call 2025 - Round 3
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-56509819 Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2025 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
In the contemporary digital landscape, where misinformation poses a serious threat, our project seeks to achieve different
objectives aimed at empowering youth to effectively navigate the complexities of the digital era. The primary goal is to
enhance the digital literacy and critical thinking skills of participants, providing them with the tools necessary to critically
evaluate information encountered in the digital realm. We believe it is important to distinguish between reliable and
misleading content, fostering a generation of digitally savvy individuals. Furthermore, our project aims to delve into the
intricacies of content marketing, manipulative algorithms and the role of social media in disseminating information.
Understanding these concepts is crucial for participants to grasp the mechanisms behind the spread of misinformation and
to develop a newer perspective on the digital landscape. The exploration of digital contracts, online scams and the evolution
of information dissemination represents another dimension of our project. Participants will gain insights into the implications
of digital contracts and privacy policies, while also learning to identify and counteract online scams. Understanding the
historical transition from traditional media to the dominance of social media platforms is integral to comprehending the
accelerated spread and impact of misinformation. A key focus of our project is to combat fake news, conspiracy theories
and information overload. Strategies will be developed to identify and counteract misinformation and participants will be
equipped to navigate the challenges posed by an overwhelming influx of information, which is crucial for decision-making
and mental well-being. Our project envisions participants not only as individuals with enhanced skills but also as responsible
digital citizens actively contributing to their local communities. The emphasis on fostering a sense of responsibility in the
digital space aligns with our overarching goal of creating a safe and inclusive environment. By instilling values of ethical
behaviour and responsible sharing, we aim to inspire participants to play an active role in countering misinformation.
Furthermore, Misinformation in the Digital Era project targets to deliver internationally participating young individuals
including people living with less opportunities together. The project will target international collaborations, cultural
awareness, breaking stereotypes and creating lasting friendships. Gender balance will produce a wider insight to several
subtopics. In essence, our project is driven by a recognition of the urgent need to address misinformation challenges.
Through the attainment of these objectives, we aspire to contribute to a broader societal impact by nurturing informed,
empowered and responsible youth capable of combating misinformation in the digital era.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The key activities (also included in the timetable) planned for our project on combating misinformation in the digital era are
strategically designed to equip participants with critical skills and knowledge. In their implementation we plan to use non-
formal learning techniques as outlined. The central activities include: Digital Literacy and Critical Thinking Workshops:
Participants will engage in workshops to distinguish propaganda, fake news, conspiracy theories and enhance their critical
thinking and digital literacy, focusing on evaluating online information effectively. Social Media Watch and Responsibility
Sessions: We will explore the role of social media influencers, content creators and platforms, coupled with discussions on
responsible sharing practices to combat the dissemination of misinformation. We will have an emoji workshop where
participants will carefully review their use of the ever changing communication methods Discussion Panels on Content
Marketing and Algorithms:We will have fishbowl discussions that will delve into the role of algorithms, content marketing
strategies and their contributions to the spread of misinformation. Sessions on Digital Contracts, Privacy and Online Scams:
We will do a comprehensive session on the implications of digital contracts, privacy policies including GDPR and online
scams in the context of misinformation. Expert-Led Sessions on Critique on Scientific Studies: A workshop on scientific
studies will be done to provide a unique view on what scientific studies are to be believed .It will provide a diverse and
professional perspective in relation to their quality and their role in misinformation, disinformation and malinformation.
Interactive Exploration of Information Evolution: We will have discussions to investigate the historical transition from
traditional media to the dominance of social media platforms and its impact on the evolution of misinformation. Strategies to
Combat Fake News and Conspiracy Theories: We will have a session to develop strategies to identify and counteract fake
news and conspiracy theories, encouraging active participation and critical analysis. Creating a Safe and Inclusive
Environment/Online ethics:We will have a session where participants will talk about the social media ethics safe through
discussions on mutual respect, punctuality and active listening. Our activities will progress in the following order as will be in
the timetable attached: Commencing with icebreaker games on the arrival day, our program fosters a welcoming
environment, adhering to the principle of inclusivity. The first day, following the introductory phase, is dedicated to
presenting the project, acquainting participants with the Youthpass and initiating discussions on the learning agreement. A
team-building activity serves as a unique approach to building connections, promoting friendships and understanding
individual strengths and weaknesses. To ensure the well-being of participants, we’ll have a Treasure hunt session in the
city. The next day will encompass sessions on business strategies to combat fake news and conspiracy theories,
emphasising active participation and critical analysis. Creating a safe and inclusive environment is a constant theme

Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-56509819 Deadline (Brussels Time) 01 Oct 2025 12:00:00
throughout, with discussions on mutual respect, punctuality and active listening. The number of participants will be 35, 5
participants (18-30 years of age) including one group leader (min. 18 years of age), a total seven coming from each
countries. There will be two facilitators from Hungary to manage the project during the sessions and also help the people in
need. The participants for this project include young individuals with diverse backgrounds and fewer opportunities ranging
from students to young professionals, who have a genuine interest in media, technology, critical thinking and addressing
societal challenges. We will intentionally select participants from diverse European regions, including those from
disadvantaged or crisis-affected areas. This deliberate approach aims to bridge the gap by providing equal opportunities for
skill development and knowledge acquisition. Participation in the project will also be gender balanced to ensure
proportionality and equal representation. Participants are expected to be digital enthusiasts actively engaged with online
platforms and demonstrate motivation to combat misinformation. The project not only aims to equip participants with
essential skills but also fosters cross-cultural awareness through an international exchange format, creating an environment
where open-minded learners can collaboratively contribute to tackling the complexities of misinformation in the digital age.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
In our mission to combat misinformation in the digital era, our project sets forth ambitious objectives designed to empower
participants with essential skills and insights. The core aim is to cultivate a robust understanding of critical thinking and
digital literacy, enabling participants to be thoroughly equipped to navigate the intricate landscape of online information.
Drawing inspiration from our past experiences in youth exchanges, we recognize the necessity for impactful and engaging
activities, ensuring that the program remains effective. Understanding the various aspects of the topic will empower
participants to make informed decisions and contribute to a culture of thoughtful information dissemination.. As such,
participants will develop and gain an understanding of digital literacy skills. Participants will be better equipped with skills for
managing their content for example advertisements and sponsored content, as well as personal websites and blogs.
Organisations will experience increased visibility and promotional opportunities, attracting more and new interest in their
endeavours and activities. Consequently, they stand to gain new clientele from both the local community and international
collaborations, thereby supplementing their overall outreach and impact. Through dissemination of the project, partner
organisations will extend their reach to more young people, opening avenues for future opportunities and collaborations
hence developing their international presence for further projects. Which in turn promotes the topic, Erasmus+ as well as the
European Union values. In addition, a successful execution of the project is envisioned to strengthen the relationships
between partner organizations, fostering personal connections and ensuring efficient and transparent communication
channels. The knowledge gained during the youth exchange is anticipated to be valuable for the partner organizations
themselves as they will post the learning outcomes on SALTO for other NGOs to learn from the prepared material.
Participants through their personal conversations with other young people, social media engagement and presentations at
local events will get to be more active in the fight against misinformation. This engagement not only creates awareness but
also inspires entrepreneurship among peers, encouraging more young individuals to explore Erasmus+ initiatives. Local
communities, including parents and educators, benefit from the knowledge and skills disseminated by project participants.
Local businesses also gain hence financially improving the living standards of the locals. Besides friendship, building
networks is another significant outcome, which will foster stable connections between participating organisations and even
participants. This network may become the potential foundation for future collaborations and co-operations for other projects
addressing misinformation and promoting digital literacy.

Background and experience
Please briefly present your group. Please describe also how the group was created.
Our informal group is Youth Avenue, based in Budapest, Hungary. We are a vibrant group of young people, both local and
international students, who came together to promote unity, cultural understanding and personal growth. We create
opportunities for young people who would like to try and learn something new and gain some experience and knowledge for
personal and professional development. A critical priority for us revolves around cultivating an informed youth, as we firmly
acknowledge the strength of knowledge. Our objective is to facilitate the accessibility of information to the younger
generation, fostering their intellectual growth. Our informal group operates with the core values of the European Union,
including:
● Unity: We believe in the strength of diversity and the power of coming together to create positive change.
● Solidarity: We stand in solidarity with each other, supporting one another's growth and well-being.
● Equality: We advocate for equal opportunities and rights for all individuals, regardless of their background or identity.
● Inclusion: We strive to create an inclusive environment where everyone feels valued and respected.
At Youth Avenue, we organise a variety of local activities, including excursions and integration events, where members can
connect, explore Budapest and create lasting friendships. Additionally, we actively participate in Erasmus+ projects. We are
focusing on the themes of digitalization, access to information and fostering connections between Hungary, Europe and
Africa. We would like to operate a youth centre in Homa Bay, Kenya in the close future with our local and international
partners as well. That’s why - also in the close future - we will make a registered foundation from our informal group. We are
already in active talks with our Kenya and USA based partners. Through this cooperation we will be able to bring closer the
cultural relations between Hungary and Kenya. We plan it based on EU values matching small researches from our and
partners’ members as well as active social media presence. At this stage (until we are not registered officially, we don’t have
social media just background work in progress).
Our initiatives are focused around youth development, aiming to empower young people to reach their full potential. We
place a strong emphasis on cultural awareness and education, providing opportunities for members to learn about different
cultures and traditions in the process breaking harmful stereotypes.
Does your group have any experience with youth work? if yes Please describe such experience.
We offer activities tailored to international mobilities, covering a wide range of topics related to youth development,
digitalization and cultural exchange. Whether it's through workshops, seminars, or collaborative projects, we strive to create
meaningful experiences that broaden perspectives and enrich lives.
Our local activities so far:
(cid:0) Dissemination activities locally for Erasmus+ funded projects.
(cid:0) Cooperation with Square of Youth Association and G.A.F.O.R.D. regarding international Erasmus+ mobilities as well as
for the Kenya based youth centre planning.
(cid:0) Board game nights by keeping members active through networking.
(cid:0) Excursions in Hungary to explore more places and the local culture.
Please describe the profile of each of the group members and what does each one bring to the project (in terms of
competences, experience etc.).
Legal Representative – Kristóf Veres
- Former Ambassador to Hungary for the European Student Think Tank(EST)
- Facilitator at Effective Altruism(EA) Hungary Fellowships
- Student Mentor at HOOK Budapest University of Technology and Economics
- Former European Solidarity Corps (ESC) Volunteer
- Summer Counsellor at Camp America USA
- Participant in numerous Erasmus+ youth exchanges and training courses
- Student at BME Electrical Engineering
Contact Person – Zoltán Csiszár
- Student Mentor at International Mentor Team (IMT)
- Msc. Electrical Engineering
- Experience with various Student Organisations
- Experience in Event management
- Participant in several trainings and youth exchanges
- Interest Areas: Synergy of Technical proficiency and social awareness.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
NGO SATELLITE is a non-governmental organization dedicated to empowering and supporting young people aged 13-35
to actively participate in their communities, foster personal development, and contribute to positive social change. Key
Objectives of the organisation: 1.Education and Skill Development: Provide educational programs and workshops to
develop essential life skills, including leadership, communication, and critical thinking. 2.Youth Advocacy: Advocate for the
rights and concerns of young people at local, national, and international levels, ensuring their voices are heard in decision-
making processes. 3.Community Engagement: Organize community-based projects and initiatives encouraging youth
engagement, volunteerism, and collaboration with local stakeholders. 4.Health and Well-being: Address issues related to the
physical and mental well-being of young individuals through awareness campaigns, health workshops, and support
networks. 5.Cultural Exchange and Diversity: Facilitate cultural exchange programs to promote understanding, tolerance,
and appreciation of diversity among young people. 6.Career Guidance and Entrepreneurship: Offer career guidance and
entrepreneurship programs to assist young people in making informed choices about their future and encourage innovation.
7.Social Innovation: Support and incubate social innovation projects initiated by young people that address local challenges
and contribute to sustainable development. 8.Digital Literacy: Promote digital literacy and technology skills to ensure that
young people are equipped to navigate the digital world and harness its potential for positive impact. 9.Networking and
Collaboration: Build a network of young leaders, professionals, and organizations to foster collaboration, knowledge sharing,
and mentorship opportunities. NGO SATELLITE is the member of international network GYBN and coordinator of the
national section of the JEF-Europe in Ukraine. The organisation is the candidate for joining in National Youth Council of
Ukraine.
What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
Environmental youth trainings “Eco Lifestyle” This project is tailored to the dynamic energy of young minds and combines
the fundamentals of ecology with practical aspects of sustainable living. Participants can delve into interactive sessions that
explore the intricate balance of ecosystems, biodiversity, and the impact of human lifestyles on the environment. The
program is structured to help young individuals understand the importance of environmental conservation and how they can
contribute to it. Our program's focus on sustainability aims to foster a sense of responsibility and ownership toward the
environment among young individuals. The course of meetings about SDG Our dynamic course of 20 meetings on
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) for youth aims to empower the leaders of tomorrow. Our initiative is geared
towards the youth, covering the 17 SDGs and addressing global challenges such as poverty, inequality, and climate change.

The program goes beyond theoretical concepts, encouraging practical application and fostering a sense of responsibility.
Participants will develop the skills required to initiate local projects aligned with the SDGs, promoting a sense of global
citizenship and social responsibility. Through interactive meetings held once a fortnight, participants engage in discussions
and collaborate on projects, gaining a comprehensive understanding of the SDGs and their relevance in shaping a better
world. Youth-Up Our program focuses on working with young people and providing them with knowledge and experience to
develop essential competencies and be involved in youth work. Within the program, we worked with a group of young
people and taught them the essentials of non- formal education methods and tools, as well as how to create, plan, and
organise activities. This program has 20 successful alumni who are now involved in the youth work field on different levels.
Let’s Speak Diversity! The project aims to develop the competencies of both youth workers and activists, focusing on how
to sensitively approach, empower, include, and work with people coming from minorities and linguistic backgrounds,
including Roma youth and other Ukrainian minorities. Within the project has been organized offline and online meetings and
mentorship Eco Friends The project aimed to raise awareness about the influence of the war on the environmental situation
in Ukraine. The first stage of the project was conducting research at universities and interviewing young people. The second
stage of the project was training course in Kyiv and different regions of Ukraine with the cooperation of Red Cross Societies.
The final stage was creating a boardgame for young people to get them to know more about the situation and its
consequences. The aim of this game we have created is to share facts about the impact of the war in Ukraine, make them
use critical thinking to debate on the topic and realize each person's influence on what is happening around us. Speaking
Club 'English for All" The Speaking Club is a community-driven initiative that aims to facilitate the development of confident
and practical communication skills among individuals. Through engaging in discussions on a range of topics - from everyday
anecdotes to more complex issues - members of the club can hone their speaking skills while enjoying the process. One
notable aspect of this club is its supportive and collaborative community, which provides an environment conducive to
learning and growth. Additionally, the club offers valuable networking opportunities for members to connect with like-minded
individuals and expand their professional or personal circles. By joining this club, individuals can engage with youth from
diverse regions and collaborate on everyday tasks and exercises while pursuing their academic goals. In essence, the
Speaking Club strikes the perfect balance between learning and fun, enabling members to enhance their communication
skills and enjoy the process of personal development. Book Club "LiterNature" A book club is a community of readers who
convene to discuss literature and exchange ideas. It offers a unique opportunity to appreciate good reading and foster
camaraderie by sharing insights, thoughts, and humour. Our book club meets regularly online via Zoom once a month to
explore selected books and engage in lively discussions. Our members not only discuss characters, plots, and various
genres but also develop new friendships and exchange views on different topics. This forum serves as a platform for
discussions, debates, and intellectual challenges, giving members the confidence to articulate their opinions and viewpoints.
Furthermore, besides being a platform for intellectual discourse, the book club is also an excellent forum for social
interaction and building relationships. Our members enjoy the connection and the opportunity to discuss their favourite
books with like-minded individuals.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
Anna Bilous The CEO of SATELLITE - Anna Bilous, holds a master’s in environmental science. She has experience in the
youth field for over six years. Her solid practical expertise concerning all the aspects of the project cycle in a youth project:
need assessment, proposal writing, implementation, evaluation, follow-up activities, and reporting. She has participated in
various trainings, youth exchanges, and conferences, with solid knowledge in project management gained through the
preparation and implementation of multiple projects (Erasmus+ and EYF programs). She is also a qualified trainer and
educator; she has experience running youth projects on national and international levels, mainly on human rights, the
environment, and physical and mental health. Konstantine Gagnidze The COO of SATELLITE - Konstantine Gagnidze, is an
operation officer, project manager and youth trainer. He has experience in youth work for over three years and has actively
participated in various international and local youth projects. He is good at facilitating training and arranging other
organisational issues of the project. He has vast experience in organising local projects as well as international Erasmus +
and EYF projects. He is involved in the work of several global networks like JEF-Europe and YEE. He studies management
science and organises projects connected to entrepreneurship, career guidance, start-ups, etc. Despite this, he specialised
and worked on topics like LGBTQIA+ society, democracy, gender equality, human rights and technology. In addition, he is
coordinating the volunteers in our organisation. He has worked with the European Solidarity Corp coordinator in the direction
of sending and hosting them. NGO SATELLITE’s team Members of NGO SATELLITE are mainly devoted young people
with unique and interesting backgrounds. They have experience and are responsible for the educational content of our
activities on local and international levels. Some have previously been involved in designing and facilitating trainings and
activities carried out in the context of various Ukrainian and EU Programs. Their competencies will be used to prepare the
participants for intercultural experiences, help the group maximise the learning outputs, and assist the implementation team
in disseminating results, providing higher visibility of the project and exploitation of the results.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Teen-Turn’s mission is to combat gender and class stereotypes as well as inform participants on education and career
options. Teen-Turn does this by providing young girls aged 13-18 from underrepresented communities or from where third
level education is uncommon with free mentored after school activities, two-week summer work placements in STEM
environments, exam support and alumnae professional development programming. We work with STEM companies
particularly by having female company representatives mentor our participants, perpetuating interest and enthusiasm.
Through this, young girls are assisted in identifying themselves with STEM career environments and making informed third
level course choices which can later contribute to combatting the gender gap within the STEM sector as more women earn
STEM qualifications.
What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
In addition to our summer work placements, we run a variety of after school programmes and clubs. All our sessions are free
and supervised and run for twelve weeks taking place both online and in-person, including: Project Squad - Our Project
Squad programme invites teen girls to create a science fair project for their submissions to both the National SciFest, and
BT Young Scientist competitions that take place annually and are open to any second-level student in Ireland. The sessions

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
Under EU law, the ability to access and share information freely is recognized as a fundamental right. In addition, the United
Nations SDGs emphasize that the spread of information and communications technology and global interconnectedness
has great potential to accelerate human progress, bridge the digital divide, and develop knowledge in societies. However,
alongside these vast opportunities, we face a critical challenge: the spread of misinformation.
The issue of misinformation has evolved into one of the most pressing challenges of the contemporary digital landscape,
affecting politics, science, health, and social interactions. The 2023 Eurobarometer on Media and News found that 70% of
Europeans are worried about disinformation online, while in Hungary specifically, 65% of internet users report encountering
fake news at least once a week (European Commission, 2023). This is particularly alarming in a country where 92% of
16–24-year-olds use social media daily (Eurostat, 2022).
Hungarian examples highlight how misinformation impacts society. During the COVID-19 pandemic, false claims about
vaccines circulated widely on Hungarian Facebook groups and Telegram channels, contributing to vaccine hesitancy:
surveys in 2021 showed that only 45% of Hungarians trusted vaccines initially, one of the lowest rates in the EU (ECDC,
2021). Another example is the 2022 parliamentary elections, where fact-checking platforms like Lakmusz.hu documented
dozens of viral disinformation cases targeting young voters. These cases illustrate the growing vulnerability of Hungarian
youth to emotionally charged, misleading content.
Young people are particularly at risk. As “digital natives,” they are active users of platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and
Facebook but often lack the critical thinking skills to navigate this information landscape. A 2022 OECD report showed that
only 44% of Hungarian 15-year-olds could distinguish between fact and opinion online, which is below the EU average. This
lack of digital literacy makes them more vulnerable to misinformation, disinformation, and malinformation, as well as to the
emotional manipulation these can provoke.
Misinformation fuels divisive narratives, deepens social divides, and increases inequalities by targeting vulnerable groups. It
has been linked to hate speech, online harassment, and even real-world violence. In Hungary, NGOs such as Political
Capital have warned that conspiracy theories about migrants or LGBTQ+ people spread online have contributed to higher
levels of polarization and intolerance among youth. The risks are not only political: algorithms on platforms like TikTok and
Facebook often prioritize sensational, emotionally charged content over balanced reporting, amplifying biased or false
narratives.
Consequently, with the growing influence of social media influencers and content creators, the line between fact and fiction
has blurred. In Hungary, for example, some popular YouTubers have been criticized for spreading unverified claims about
climate change or mental health, shaping the opinions of thousands of young followers. The ease of going viral also creates
pressure on youth to share content quickly, without verifying its accuracy, which normalizes irresponsible digital practices.
Another dimension of this challenge is digital contracts and online privacy. In Hungary, research from the Nemzeti Média- és
Hírközlési Hatóság (NMHH, 2022) found that over 70% of young internet users accept cookies or terms of service without
reading them, leaving them vulnerable to scams and unethical data practices. In addition, the normalization of adult content
online has psycho-social effects on youth, including distorted perceptions of relationships and increased exposure to
exploitation.
Recognizing these challenges, our youth exchange project is not just about raising awareness—it is about empowering
young people to become informed and responsible digital citizens. Addressing misinformation requires more than passive
consumption of facts; it demands active engagement, critical analysis, and ethical responsibility in online spaces.
Our project offers young people the opportunity to develop these essential skills through experiential learning, real-life case
studies, and interactive activities. By analyzing Hungarian and European case studies (e.g., COVID-19, elections, climate
denial campaigns), participants will learn to question, investigate, and verify the content they encounter.
We are conducting this youth exchange because young people are at the heart of societal change, and their capacity to
shape the future is immense. By equipping them with tools to critically evaluate information, understand the mechanics
behind online content, and recognize the ethical implications of digital behavior, we aim to foster a generation that is resilient
against the dangers of misinformation.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Misinformation in the Digital Era’s objectives strongly align with the Erasmus programme. First and foremost, this project
aims to promote critical thinking through a non-biased approach and an in-depth exploration of the challenges posed by
misinformation, disinformation and malinformation. In addition, inclusion, equity and the concept of mobility are integrated
into our project. We also explore how misinformation has led to misunderstandings on sustainability, climate change and
environmentally friendly practices. Our project’s major theme is digital literacy and ethical responsibility, particularly
concerning the consumption and sharing of information. Misinformation often targets marginalized communities and

Organisations will benefit immediately by gaining increased visibility in both local and international contexts. By promoting
the project through their social media channels and networks, organisations can attract new volunteers and future project
participants. The Youth Avenue group in Budapest, for example, will present the project’s results at local community events
and in youth spaces, which positions the organisation as a credible actor in the field of digital literacy. Partner organisations
will also strengthen their reputation within Erasmus+ networks, making them more attractive for future collaborations.
After the project:
The results of the youth exchange will have lasting value. The outputs — such as workshop outlines, fact-checking toolkits,
and short awareness videos — will be uploaded on SALTO-Youth and partner websites, serving as reusable materials for
youth workers and NGOs across Europe. Locally, these materials can be integrated into school workshops, university clubs,
and NGO training sessions in Hungary and partner countries.
The project also fosters a pipeline of young volunteers. Experience shows that many youth exchange participants later
become active supporters of their sending organisations. We expect at least 5–10 alumni to remain engaged with Youth
Avenue and partner NGOs, helping with future events, mentoring new participants, and expanding the organisations’
outreach capacity.
Organisations will gain new competences in facilitation and digital youth work. For example, Hungarian facilitators will
experiment with “fishbowl debates” and “emoji workshops” during the exchange — methods that can then be integrated into
their regular youth activities. Similarly, partners will learn about Hungarian examples of combating misinformation (e.g.,
Lakmusz.hu fact-checking initiatives), which they can adapt to their own national contexts.
The project will also encourage cross-sector collaboration. By involving local schools, libraries, and media outlets in
dissemination, organisations will build stronger ties with community stakeholders. This creates opportunities for joint
national projects, such as integrating media literacy into school programs or hosting annual awareness days.
Finally, the inclusive and diverse approach of this project — with space for marginalized voices and participants with fewer
opportunities — will help organisations reach new target groups. In Hungary, this could mean involving international
students and minority youth in local activities, making the organisation more reflective of the community it serves. Over time,
this inclusivity strengthens the organisation’s legitimacy and sustainability.
What would be the impact of your project beyond the participants and participating organisations, at local, regional,
national, if any European level ?
Local level:
In a local level and in the participants’ hometowns, young people will actively share their experiences through school visits,
youth clubs, and presentations in local cultural centers. For example, our group regularly cooperates with the Square of
Youth Association and local NGOs, where project participants can organize interactive workshops on digital literacy for
secondary school students. We also plan local dissemination events, where young people can present simple fact-checking
tools to peers and parents.
Furthermore, participants will use their own social media networks to share outputs in Hungarian and English, ensuring
wider outreach to their communities. This engagement will not only create awareness but also inspire entrepreneurship —
for instance, some youth may participate in podcasts, YouTube explainers, or TikTok campaigns that promote responsible
online behavior. Residents, including parents and educators, benefit directly from the improved knowledge and skills of
participants. Local businesses (especially those relying on social media marketing) will also benefit indirectly, as young
people trained in responsible digital practices can contribute to healthier, more transparent communication strategies. The
findings will be also brought to Square of Youth Association as they are about to host adult education related to AI topics.
Through this opportunity we can reach with the topic a broader audience in age as well.
Regional level:
Through targeted dissemination in Hungary and neighboring regions, the project’s thematic concerns will be amplified
beyond the immediate participants. Regional youth organizations, such as those coordinated by the Tempus Public
Foundation and Erasmus+ alumni networks, will be invited to adopt our project methods in their own activities.
We also plan to participate in regional youth events, such as university community fairs, where participants will present their
results. This could raise awareness on issues like hate speech, conspiracy theories, and propaganda, while encouraging
regional youth organizations to integrate digital literacy into their programs. Over time, the project’s visibility may influence
regional policymakers to strengthen initiatives against online misinformation.
National level:
At national level, we will proactively seek media coverage via local newspapers, radio, and youth-targeted online outlets.
This amplifies the project’s reach and spreads awareness about misinformation and ethical online practices. Additionally,
motivated participants will be encouraged to join youth advisory boards (e.g., the Hungarian National Youth Council), where

Please describe the background of the participants in each participating group and how each group was formed. Please
also provide information on the group leaders, the age of the participants and how country balance is ensured. If
necessary, explain how the gender balance is respected.
We will have 35 participants in our project from 5 countries, i.e. 6 participants plus one group leader prioritising on gender
balance, striving for equal representation of male and female participants in each group. Participants in each group are
selected to ensure a rich diversity of backgrounds, experiences and studies. The aim is to create a dynamic learning
environment where participants can benefit from a broad range of perspectives. This diversity includes, but is not limited to,
students, professionals and individuals from various fields such as media, education, psychology and social sciences. This
diversity enhances the overall learning experience by fostering cross-disciplinary discussions and knowledge exchange. We

experiences, with younger participants bringing fresh insights and older participants contributing valuable experiences to the
discussions. We will have the group leaders play a crucial role in facilitating communication between their sending
organisations, participants and the host through the program. They will be selected based on their experience in youth
projects, leadership skills and understanding of the project's thematic focus. The minimum age for group leaders according
to Erasmus+ guidelines is set at 18 and since our project will tackle youth between 18 and 30, we will consider mostly their
leadership skills and experience. In addition, we will ensure country balance is important as we aim to involve a diverse
representation of various regions and cultural backgrounds. This approach will ensure a different perspective on the issues
that we will address during the youth exchange. Partner organisations will help in identifying suitable candidates,
considering their backgrounds, experiences and potential contributions to the project. The selection process will involve a
fair and transparent evaluation of applicants, with an emphasis on ensuring equal opportunities for all. To address the needs
of participants facing situations that make their involvement more challenging, at least two individuals from each partner
organisation are selected as participants with fewer opportunities. These participants may face barriers linked to cultural,
educational, economic, social challenges. Their selection process will be discreet and they are not informed that they have
been chosen based on their specific situations. Participants facing challenges, such as economic barriers, are provided with
additional support, including the option to request travel tickets. This ensures that financial constraints do not hinder their
participation in the youth exchange. During the participant selection process, special needs, including medical requirements
and dietary restrictions, are taken into account. This information is gathered confidentially and is used to make necessary
arrangements to accommodate participants' needs during the program.
Please describe the role and involvement of the participants from each participating group in all phases (planning before
submitting application, preparation, implementation of activities and follow-up).
The planning phase of the project was a collaborative effort between the project organisers and the participants from each
group. A participant point of view was adopted, involving direct communication with the participants to understand their
needs, preferences and expectations. A prior meeting was organised, where open discussions took place to gather input
from participants. During this session, participants were assured of the project team's support and their valuable input was
considered in shaping the project proposal. This meeting also served as a platform to address any concerns, clarify the
project's objectives and establish a sense of ownership among the participants. The preparation phase involves active
engagement with participants across partner countries. Clear communication channels are established to facilitate smooth
information flow and collaboration through a shared drive, padlet and a teams channel. Detailed discussions take place,
outlining the specific responsibilities of each participant and team leader. An infopack is collaboratively developed,
addressing the practical aspects of the project, such as travel arrangements, accommodation details and daily schedules.
Emphasis is placed on preparing participants for their roles and responsibilities before and during the application process.
Small details, including cultural considerations and logistical preparations, are thoroughly discussed to ensure a well-
prepared and cohesive group. Participants will play an active role during the implementation phase of the project. They will
contribute to the execution of planned activities, actively engage in output and learning material preparation and collaborate
on the creation of final project outcomes. Participants will have the opportunity to implement their own ideas related to the
program, ensuring a sense of ownership and creativity. If they would like to lead an activity for example a discussion, a
group presentation, they are added to the schedule. Energizers will be led by the participants, fostering a lively and inclusive
atmosphere. Social media presence is co-created with participants, with their consent sought for the usage of pictures and
videos. Cultural night is fully organised by participants, showcasing the diversity within the group. Free evening activities,
such as movie nights, are chosen by participants with the support of the project team. Participants will receive a Youthpass,
encouraging self-evaluation and skill development. The project provides a platform for participants to share their insights,
learnings and reflections, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of Erasmus+ and misinformation in the digital era.
This phase is designed to empower participants to actively contribute to the project's success, fostering a sense of
responsibility and teamwork. Our participants will be involved in the dissemination as they will plan out the activities they
plan to do by themselves and the organizations will only support them during this activity. In the follow-up phase,
participants will continue their active involvement and contribute to various aspects of the project's sustainability. They will
engage in social media content creation for their local NGOs and personal platforms, showcasing their experiences and
promoting the project outcomes. Participants who express interest are given the opportunity to contribute more actively,
potentially creating their own Erasmus+ youth exchange or participating in other programs. Prepared outputs from the
project are utilised during dissemination activities at home, allowing participants to share their learnings with their local
communities. The project team supports participants in further engagement with their local NGOs and communities,
fostering a continued sense of social responsibility. Participants are encouraged to explore additional projects related to
Erasmus+ and beyond, creating a pathway for ongoing personal and community development.

Information literacy: They will learn to critically assess information sources by practicing with real case studies (e.g. COVID-
19 vaccine disinformation in Hungary, election-related fake news from 2022). This includes distinguishing between reliable
and misleading content, recognizing “clickbait” headlines, and identifying manipulated images or videos.
Media literacy: They will understand the mechanisms of misinformation across media formats — written articles, social
media posts, memes, short-form videos — by analyzing how algorithms on TikTok, Instagram, and Facebook amplify
sensational content.
Digital literacy: They will explore how to navigate online spaces safely and responsibly, including recognizing phishing
attempts, understanding privacy settings, and learning to protect personal data. For example, participants will review the
Hungarian Media Authority’s (NMHH) 2022 research showing that 70% of young people accept cookies without reading
terms, and they will discuss safer alternatives.
Sustainability link: They will investigate the environmental impact of misinformation, e.g., how “climate change denial”
campaigns spread online, and how fake sustainability claims (greenwashing) mislead consumers. This connects
environmental issues with digital narratives and promotes eco-friendly choices.
Health and well-being: They will learn about the psychological effects of misinformation, such as anxiety, panic, or
polarization. Participants will practice mindfulness strategies for responsible media consumption and reflect on how
disinformation during COVID-19 affected mental health in Hungary and Europe.
Skills:
Critical thinking: By engaging in debates, role plays, and “fact-checking labs,” they will practice questioning sources,
spotting logical inconsistencies, and analyzing arguments.
Communication: Through structured debates and group presentations, participants will refine their ability to express ideas
clearly, counter misinformation respectfully, and adapt messages for different audiences (e.g. peers vs. parents).
English and intercultural competence: Since English will be the working language, participants will naturally improve their
language skills. In addition, through intercultural workshops and culture nights, they will explore how misinformation varies
across countries (e.g. stereotypes about migrants in Hungary vs. Ireland), developing empathy and global awareness.
Collaboration and teamwork: Non-formal methods (problem-solving tasks, simulation games, small-group challenges) will
strengthen cooperation, negotiation, critical thinking, and collective decision-making.
Civic competence: Through case studies (e.g. how misinformation influenced Hungary’s election campaigns or attitudes
toward minorities), participants will understand the societal consequences of disinformation and reflect on their own
responsibility in democratic participation.
Entrepreneurial skills: Participants will be challenged to design small-scale campaigns or creative tools (e.g. a TikTok video
series, infographic, or podcast idea) to counter misinformation. This supports innovation, leadership, and project
management skills.
Attitudes/Behaviours:
Responsible digital citizens: They will develop the habit of verifying content before sharing, becoming more cautious about
spreading unverified news.
Ethical awareness: They will become more aware of online privacy, consent, and the dangers of manipulation, choosing
responsible online behaviours.
Cultural openness: By exploring stereotypes and misinformation across cultures, they will cultivate empathy and respect for
diversity.
Resilience: By understanding how misinformation spreads and manipulates emotions, participants will become more
resistant to polarization, hate speech, and conspiracy theories.
Concrete outputs of learning:
A set of youth-created digital guidelines (“10 Tips for Spotting Fake News”) in multiple languages.
Mini campaigns (videos, graphics, blog posts) created during workshops and shared on partner organisations’ social media.
Personal action plans where each participant outlines how they will apply new skills in their local communities (e.g. school
presentations, NGO volunteering, or peer education).
What are the basic elements of the activity? Please describe at the very least the venue(s), non formal learning methods
used, aims of the session etc.
The activities, also included in the timetable, will encompass engaging workshops in the topics of fake news, role of
misinformation on sustainability & climate change, propaganda, stereotypes and will employ participatory methods like
challenges to identify deep fakes, false information together with presentations encouraging active involvement and
fostering critical thinking. Workshops will explore topics such as media literacy, information verification and digital literacy.
We will also use interactive simulations to recreate real-life scenarios related to misinformation, allowing participants to
navigate challenges and develop practical skills in identifying and countering false information. The "Viral Impact Simulation"
activity will immerse participants in a controlled environment where they create and share content to observe the dynamics
of going viral. We aim for our participants to learn about environmentally friendly practices and the role misinformation
across the internet and social media has had to play in perpetuating narratives such as climate change is a hoax. Through
this we hope to change the attitudes of not only our participants but also the stakeholders and general public. Through
reflections, group discussions and case studies, participants gain insights into the positive and negative consequences of
content going viral, fostering digital competence, media literacy and responsible communication skills. In addition, we will
have structured debates and open discussions on ethical social media practices to encourage participants to express
diverse viewpoints, promoting dialogue and the exchange of ideas. The aim is to enhance communication skills and promote
a deeper understanding of the complexities of misinformation. Participants will also engage in role-playing exercises during

the role of culture in shaping perceptions. The venue of the activities will be in Balatonmáriafürdő, Hungary, that features

projectors and Wi-Fi to ensure smooth implementation of digital learning methods. Access to reliable internet connectivity

How will the groups of participants cooperate and communicate between them to prepare and follow-up on the Youth

Before the start of the youth exchange, an online meeting will be convened for all participants to establish ground rules,
discuss expectations, divide the tasks among participants and team leaders and provide an overview of the project. This
meeting will be facilitated through Zoom or Google Meet. For the initial communication channels, formal and legal
documents will be disseminated via email for clarity and documentation purposes. Simultaneously, dedicated WhatsApp
and Facebook groups will be created to serve as primary channels for day-to-day communication, quick updates and
announcements. The team leaders will play a pivotal role in facilitating these discussions. During the Project: Each national
group will have its WhatsApp subgroup for specific announcements and coordination with the group leaders serving as
admins. Online meetings, facilitated through platforms like Zoom, will be scheduled to discuss progress, challenges and
upcoming activities with the sending partners. For collaborative efforts, a Padlet board will be established for information
sharing and brainstorming, while a shared Google Drive folder will facilitate document collection. In-person meetings during
physical gatherings will also be incorporated to strengthen interpersonal bonds, although online channels will remain the
primary means of communication so the information flow can be tracked such as sharings and votings. After: After the youth
exchange, dissemination of outcomes is crucial. Hence, there is a timeslot scheduled in the timetable for the participants to
discuss dissemination and which platform to use. Social media platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, TikTok and X will
be used to share project outcomes, key learnings and success stories. Periodic Facebook newsletters summarising the
project's achievements and impact will be distributed to all participants, partners and relevant stakeholders. Additionally, the
organisations in participants' home countries, along with team leaders, will actively contribute to disseminating project
outcomes within their local communities through further online or physical meetings. A combination of email and social
media platforms will be employed for this purpose. The participants will also organise local dissemination activities to share
their experiences and freshly gained knowledge about Erasmus+ opportunities and the digital misinformation topic.

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
In anticipation of the youth exchange project on "Misinformation in the Digital Era," we have outlined a comprehensive plan
to ensure the well-being and effective engagement of participants throughout the entire project lifecycle. Before the Activity:
Prior to departure, participants will establish connections through social media platforms like Facebook or WhatsApp,
facilitated by our partners during pre-departure meetings. These meetings will serve as a platform to deliver critical
information about the project's objectives, participants' roles and the potential benefits they can derive. An information pack
will be distributed to provide detailed insights into project activities, objectives, goals and practical information about the
hosting country, covering needed aspects such as costs, travel, insurance, visa, accommodation and security. To enhance
the preparatory phase, workshops on intercultural communication and linguistic basics will be conducted to foster cultural
sensitivity and effective communication among participants. Additionally, comprehensive risk-prevention training will ensure
participants are well-versed in recognizing and mitigating risks associated with online activities, including a thorough review
of insurance coverage. Attention to dietary concerns, preferences and allergies will create an inclusive and safe
environment. During the Activity: Throughout the project, daily reflection sessions will be included and guided to provide
participants with a platform for processing learning experiences and sharing insights. Mentorship and support will be a key
focus, with assigned buddies for personalised guidance and regular check-ins to address concerns. Diverse learning
formats, such as workshops, group projects and interactive sessions, will cater to varied learning styles. After the Activity:
Post-activity workshops will be organised to review and support learning, discuss post-activity experiences and explore
practical strategies for applying acquired skills in participants' daily lives. A digital resource hub will be created, offering
participants access to project materials, additional readings and relevant resources for continued learning. Furthermore, an
alumni network will be established, fostering continued connection and collaboration among past participants. This network
will provide opportunities for alumni to contribute to future projects, share experiences and actively participate in ongoing
initiatives. As the participants will help to disseminate the project results through the outputs and final outcomes of the
project that they prepared, they can organise local dissemination activities physically with their peers and local communities,
so the experience and the topic will be shared with others. The national team’s team leaders and local organisations and
informal groups will support them in their implementation and reporting process of each activity that they organise including
the online content sharing. This integrated approach aims to not only prepare participants for the project but also support
them comprehensively before, during and after the activity, ensuring a meaningful and impactful experience addressing the
challenges of misinformation in the digital era. Team leaders will have a key role not just in communication related matters
but also as risk prevention within their national teams especially during the implementation phase. Before that they will
support with the proper preparation the participants, so they can be ready and fully active during the exchange, also their
learning experience and goals can meet. The hosting and partner organizations will rely on the team leaders in several
matters as the representatives of the organizations and their participants. They will maintain active communication and raise
any kind of matter to the organizers.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Ensuring the safety and well-being of participants is a top priority, and we have developed a comprehensive security plan
that covers all stages of the project. With the support of our partner organizations, we will implement strict safety protocols
before, during, and after the exchange to create a secure, inclusive, and enriching experience for all. Before departure, all
participants will attend a mandatory pre-departure workshop, where they will receive detailed safety briefings on emergency
protocols, cultural adaptation, and responsible behavior. The workshop will cover risk awareness, local emergency services
(police, hospitals, embassies), and travel safety. Team leaders will conduct individual check-ins with participants to assess
any medical, psychological, or security concerns. A confidential health questionnaire will be collected to identify medical
conditions, allergies, or specific needs, ensuring appropriate support during the exchange. A comprehensive risk
assessment will be conducted in collaboration with local partners before participants’ arrival, identifying safety challenges
and mitigation measures. Upon arrival, participants will be formally introduced to safety guidelines, emergency contacts, and
designated safe zones. The accommodation will meet high security and hygiene standards, featuring lockable rooms,
controlled access, and proximity to medical facilities. Participants will be housed in gender-appropriate rooms, with at least
two per room to ensure support in case of emergencies. Facilitators will stay nearby to provide on-site assistance if needed.
Health precautions are a priority. All participants must have valid medical insurance, such as the European Health Insurance
Card (EHIC) or equivalent. First aid kits will be available at all activity sites, and trained first aid responders will be
designated among facilitators and team leaders. Local healthcare providers, pharmacies, and hospitals will be identified
before the exchange, and participants will receive guidance on how to access medical assistance. To ensure a safe,
respectful, and inclusive environment, all participants will adhere to a strict code of conduct, which will be shared before
arrival. The code will outline expectations regarding behavior, respect for cultural diversity, and inclusion. It will prohibit
harassment, discrimination, bullying, drug and alcohol abuse, and any inappropriate conduct. A zero-tolerance policy will be
enforced, and participants will be encouraged to report concerns confidentially. Designated safeguarding officers will be
available to handle complaints sensitively and efficiently. Emergency preparedness is fully integrated into the project. A
detailed emergency response plan will cover scenarios such as medical emergencies, missing participants, fire evacuation,
and natural disasters. Regular safety briefings and emergency drills will reinforce participant preparedness. The project

info sessions where participants can share their experiences, learnings and present the enterprises developed during the

volunteering projects that further explore cultural diversity, ethical fashion or sustainability. The dissemination efforts will
extend to target a broader audience, especially those unfamiliar with the possibilities offered by programs like Erasmus+ or

Recognition of learning outcomes

Participants engage in discussions about their expectations, the project's aims, and the broader context of Erasmus+. Early
introduction to the mission of the Erasmus+ program, the Youthpass framework, and key competences helps participants
understand the practical value of their learning experience and how they can track their personal and professional
development throughout the exchange. This structured approach ensures that participants do not merely engage in the
activities but actively reflect on their growth, skill development, and the competences they acquire during the mobility. To
reinforce the learning process from the very beginning, the Youthpass framework will be introduced not as a mere
certification tool but as an essential instrument for recognizing non-formal learning. Participants will be guided in self-
assessment from day one to help them acknowledge their strengths, areas for improvement, and progress throughout the
exchange. To support this, facilitators will organize interactive introduction sessions where participants set personal learning
goals based on the key competences outlined in Youthpass. These learning goals will serve as reference points for self-
reflection and group evaluations throughout the exchange. A key component of the learning process is continuous
documentation and reflection. To make learning tangible and relatable, participants will be encouraged to use creative and
engaging methods to document their progress daily. They will have the opportunity to create digital diaries, maintain video
logs, or write blog posts to articulate their experiences, lessons learned, and key takeaways from the sessions. Additionally,
social media engagement will be leveraged as an interactive tool for reflection, where participants can post insights, creative
content, and photos that encapsulate the core messages of each activity. This not only serves as a personal record of their
learning journey but also contributes to the broader dissemination of project results. Reflection sessions will be
systematically integrated into the daily schedule to ensure that learning is actively recognized and internalized. Each day will
conclude with structured reflection activities, facilitated through small group discussions, guided journaling, and thematic
storytelling exercises where participants share personal insights from the day’s activities. These sessions will not only allow
participants to consolidate their understanding but also enable facilitators to track learning trends and make real-time
adjustments to better meet participants' needs. In addition to individual reflections, peer feedback will play a crucial role in
recognizing learning achievements. Participants will engage in collaborative activities such as mind mapping, mood board
creation, and even thematic fashion designs that visually represent their understanding of key topics. Regular peer-to-peer
evaluations will provide constructive feedback on group projects, enhancing participants’ ability to recognize their strengths,
receive insights from others, and refine their ideas. Halfway through the mobility, a structured mid-term evaluation will be
conducted to assess participants’ progress toward their learning goals. This checkpoint will allow them to reflect on their
experiences, reassess their learning objectives, and make any necessary adjustments to maximize their development.
Facilitators will also employ experiential learning techniques that challenge participants to apply their knowledge in real-
world contexts. Workshops will be followed by hands-on implementation exercises where participants will put theoretical
concepts into practice. Case studies, simulations, and role-playing activities will allow them to engage with complex issues
dynamically, reinforcing key skills such as critical thinking, problem-solving, and teamwork. As the project progresses,
reflection will become deeper and more analytical. Towards the conclusion of the exchange, a final structured reflection
session will encourage participants to synthesize their learning experiences into actionable insights they can carry forward
beyond the exchange. This process will culminate in the completion of their Youthpass certificates, where they will detail the
competences they have gained and how these skills can be applied in their personal, academic, and professional lives. The

celebration. Each participant’s journey, growth, and contributions will be acknowledged, reinforcing their achievements and
boosting their confidence in applying these newly acquired competences in future endeavors. To ensure long-term impact,
participants will be encouraged to share their learning outcomes in their local communities through presentations, blog
articles, or small projects that raise awareness of digital literacy and misinformation.

Our project embraces participants from diverse backgrounds. Our goal extends beyond social inclusion to enrich the

cultural differences may pose challenges, we are dedicated to implementing measures that support the participation of
individuals facing these barriers. We intentionally select participants from diverse European regions, including those from
disadvantaged or crisis-affected areas. This deliberate approach aims to bridge the gap by providing equal opportunities for
skill development and knowledge acquisition. 1. Barriers linked to Education and Training Systems We will organize a pre-
mobility session with participants and give them insights on the topics and thematic concerns the project will touch on. This
session will help boost the participants knowledge and clarify any doubts they may have. We will also provide pre-exchange
training materials in a variety of formats (videos, infographics and easy-to-read documents) to ensure that all participants

orientation sessions that focus on intercultural communication, cultural sensitivity and the importance of respecting diverse
perspectives. Participants will be encouraged to share their cultural norms and traditions, fostering a deeper understanding
among the group In the case of language barriers, we will ensure that materials are translated and if necessary, a translator

mentors will check in with them regularly to ensure they are comfortable, included and engaged in activities. Participants
who have experienced social exclusion may need safe spaces to express themselves and their concerns. We will create
specific times during the program for reflection and feedback, where individuals can share their experiences in a non-
judgmental environment, ensuring their voices are heard and any issues addressed. 5. Geographical barriers Partner
organisations will make sure during the preparation that they can be fully involved at all stages regardless their distance
from the project venue or sending organisations. 6. Barriers linked to discrimination The creation of small teams for
collaborative projects, such as team building activities, takes into consideration diverse backgrounds. This approach
encourages the exchange of ideas and mutual support among participants with varied perspectives. Our main objective is to
create a safe and inclusive environment where participants, irrespective of their background, feel comfortable sharing their
perspectives and seeking assistance when needed. Group leaders will play a pivotal role in supporting their members and
team activities are strategically formed to leverage the strengths of each participant. By the conclusion of the project, we
aspire for participants from less privileged backgrounds to feel empowered and capable, transcending the challenges they

their local communities, contributing to a broader societal impact in the fight against misinformation.

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
Our project is firmly committed to integrating sustainable and environmentally friendly practices throughout its
implementation, ensuring that participants engage not only with misinformation in the digital era but also develop a deeper
understanding of sustainability and environmental responsibility. Recognising the urgent need to combat climate change
and promote eco-conscious behaviours, we embed green practices at every stage, making sustainability a core element of
both project operations and educational activities. A key aspect of our approach is addressing the intersection of
misinformation and climate change. We will organise a workshop exploring how false information, climate denial campaigns,
and misleading media distort public understanding of environmental issues. Participants will critically assess social media
algorithms, stakeholder interests, and how misinformation shapes public perception. Through case studies, media literacy
exercises, and discussions, they will develop skills to evaluate environmental claims, identify credible sources, and counter
misleading narratives. This will empower them to advocate for responsible climate action in their communities, strengthening
their role as informed digital citizens. Beyond educational activities, the project’s operational framework aligns with
sustainability principles. From the outset, we will engage participants in discussions on minimising our ecological footprint
and encourage long-term eco-friendly habits. Our commitment to reducing paper waste is reflected in the use of digital
resources, ensuring that all communications, learning materials, and documentation are shared electronically. This
approach promotes digital literacy, efficiency, and a reduced environmental impact. Participants will take an active role in
sustainable practices throughout the project. They will be encouraged to implement responsible waste management, sort
recyclables, minimise waste, and eliminate single-use plastics. Reusable water bottles, utensils, and eco-friendly stationery
will be promoted, and we will collaborate with local suppliers to ensure the availability of sustainable alternatives.
Sustainable catering will be prioritised, focusing on locally sourced, seasonal, and plant-based food options to reduce the
carbon footprint associated with food production. The project will also highlight the issue of food waste, addressing its
environmental consequences and the role of misinformation in shaping unsustainable consumption patterns. To deepen
participants’ understanding of sustainability, we will introduce a session on greenwashing—the practice of companies
falsely promoting themselves as eco-friendly. This session will equip participants with the skills to critically analyse
corporate environmental claims, distinguish between genuine and misleading sustainability initiatives, and foster ethical
consumerism. Through real-world examples and interactive discussions, participants will learn to identify truly sustainable
business models and consider how they can apply these insights in their own communities. Transportation choices will also
reflect our commitment to sustainability (green travel). We will encourage green travel options such as trains, carpooling,
and other low-carbon transport methods whenever feasible. This aligns with the EU’s sustainability goals while also raising
awareness about the environmental impact of travel. Additionally, we will implement energy conservation measures,
ensuring that venues and accommodations follow energy-efficient practices, including LED lighting, reducing unnecessary
energy consumption, and minimising water waste. Raising awareness of sustainable practices will be a central component
of the project. Participants will engage in reflection activities to assess their environmental impact and identify personal
actions for adopting more sustainable habits. Interactive workshops, peer-to-peer discussions, and sustainability challenges
will provide practical insights into environmental responsibility. Social media campaigns and blog posts created by
participants will further amplify the project’s message, spreading knowledge on sustainable living and responsible digital
citizenship to a wider audience. Ultimately, sustainability is not just an add-on to our project but a fundamental component
that aligns with its broader themes of misinformation, critical thinking, and ethical responsibility. By embedding eco-friendly
practices into both the content and logistics, we aim to cultivate a sustainability mindset among participants. This will equip
them with the tools to navigate environmental misinformation, adopt responsible behaviours, and advocate for lasting
positive change. The impact will extend beyond the project’s duration, inspiring youth-led initiatives across Europe to
embrace sustainability and critical engagement with environmental discourse.
